emergent_themes.description: Students who approach food decisions with systematic, quantified thinking - treating food delivery as a system to be optimized rather than an emotional choice
How receiving subscriptions for free (through promotions, bundles, student deals) creates artificially positive brand perceptions and loyalty
Frequent breakfast skipping as a normalized student behavior, often rationalized as routine despite health awareness

coding_summary: P012 presents as a highly systematic, deal-optimizing student who treats food delivery like a system to be hacked. Key findings include strong multi-platform arbitrage behavior, significant say-do gaps between claimed and actual eating patterns (especially breakfast skipping), and the powerful influence of free subscriptions on brand perception. His high disposable income (£150-200/week) allows him to be deal-seeking rather than truly price-sensitive. The diary data reveals more chaotic actual behavior than his controlled self-presentation suggests.
